segments.text: Here is the opening move. Let's start by opening Overpass Turbo and load the map + query editor workspace. This gives the whole demo a place to stand.
segments.fallback_text: We start by opening Overpass Turbo and load the map + query editor workspace. That gives the workflow a stage.
segments.subtitle: Step 1: Open Overpass Turbo and load the map + query editor workspace

segments.text: We will hold for a beat here and let the screen settle before we move on.
segments.fallback_text: We will let the page catch up.
segments.subtitle: We will hold for a beat here and let the screen settle before we move on.

segments.text: With the setup in place, we can chase the signal. We split view. Now the logic and the result can share the frame.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we split view. Now both sides of the workflow are visible.
segments.subtitle: Step 2: Split view: query editor on left, map + controls on right

segments.text: Now the target comes into focus. We write a Hyderabad hospitals query in Overpass QL using a fixed city bounding box. Making the logic visible is what turns a demo into a recipe.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we write a Hyderabad hospitals query in Overpass QL using a fixed. Now the logic has a voice.
segments.subtitle: Step 3: Write a Hyderabad hospitals query in Overpass QL using a fixed city bounding box

segments.text: This is where the useful part starts. We run query to load all mapped hospitals as explorable markers. Now the page gets to do the heavy lifting.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we run query to load all mapped hospitals as explorable markers. Here the setup turns into results.
segments.subtitle: Step 4: Run query to load all mapped hospitals as explorable markers

segments.text: With the setup in place, we can chase the signal. We use 'zoom to data' to jump map viewport directly onto Hyderabad results.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we use 'zoom to data' to jump map viewport directly onto Hyderabad.
segments.subtitle: Step 5: Use 'zoom to data' to jump map viewport directly onto Hyderabad results

segments.text: Now the page has something to say. We click map markers to inspect feature tags like name, address, and amenity type.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we click map markers to inspect feature tags like name, address, and.
segments.subtitle: Step 6: Click map markers to inspect feature tags like name, address, and amenity type

segments.text: This is where the pattern starts to show. We open Data tab to inspect raw tabular output and OSM attributes.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we open Data tab to inspect raw tabular output and OSM attributes.
segments.subtitle: Step 7: Open Data tab to inspect raw tabular output and OSM attributes

segments.text: Here the workflow stops posing and starts proving. We switch back to Map view to visually compare coverage across neighborhoods.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we switch back to Map view to visually compare coverage across neighborhoods.
segments.subtitle: Step 8: Switch back to Map view to visually compare coverage across neighborhoods

segments.text: Now the page has something to say. We replace with EV charging station query to analyze clean-mobility readiness. Same rhythm, new question, and that is the whole point.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we replace with EV charging station query to analyze clean-mobility readiness. Same flow, different question.
segments.subtitle: Step 9: Replace with EV charging station query to analyze clean-mobility readiness

segments.text: This is where the pattern starts to show. EV charging points are now on the map. We compare spread versus healthcare locations. Now the comparison is easy to read.
segments.fallback_text: Now the new results are on the map for comparison.
segments.subtitle: Step 10: EV charging points rendered; now compare spread versus healthcare locations

segments.text: Here the workflow stops posing and starts proving. We run school query to map education infrastructure in the same city boundary. This is where all the quiet setup finally earns applause.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we run school query to map education infrastructure in the same city. Here the setup turns into results.
segments.subtitle: Step 11: Run school query to map education infrastructure in the same city boundary

segments.text: Now the payoff is close enough to see. We add pharmacy query as a fourth dataset to build a richer city-services comparison. One more layer makes the picture richer without making it messy.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we add pharmacy query as a fourth dataset to build a richer. Now the picture gets fuller.
segments.subtitle: Step 12: Add pharmacy query as a fourth dataset to build a richer city-services comparison

segments.text: Hmm... little pause here. The page is still catching up, so we give it a second.
segments.fallback_text: Give this a second to settle.
segments.subtitle: Hmm... little pause here. The page is still catching up, so we give it a second.

segments.text: This is where the result starts to feel earned. We single editor workflow lets you iterate quickly across multiple urban datasets. It keeps the flow easy to follow without sounding like a checklist.
segments.fallback_text: Single editor workflow lets you iterate quickly across multiple urban datasets.
segments.subtitle: Step 13: Single editor workflow lets you iterate quickly across multiple urban datasets

segments.text: From here, the workflow turns into something reusable. We open Export options for downstream GIS and analytics workflows.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we open Export options for downstream GIS and analytics workflows.
segments.subtitle: Step 14: Open Export options for downstream GIS and analytics workflows

segments.text: Now the payoff is close enough to see. We export supports GeoJSON, KML, GPX, and raw OSM for different tools.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we export supports GeoJSON, KML, GPX, and raw OSM for different tools.

segments.text: That closes the loop cleanly. The flow is ready to replay.
